message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Tavily's SOTA claim is real but its public write-up contains no usable composition mechanism — it is an engineering/token-efficiency story, not a report-writing story. Do not spend more time on it.
DETAIL: tavily-research = 52.44 on the legacy board (comp 52.84 / insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / read 49.21 — note readability is its weakest dim and it is absent from the GPT-5.5 board). The blog's substantive claims are all about retrieval and context economy: Advanced Search returns 'only the most relevant content chunks from each source'; 'global state persistence and source deduplication' to keep the agent on fresh info and detect when 'the information scope is narrowing'; 66% token reduction vs Open Deep Research; 'guard-railed freedom' for the LLM to iterate. On composition it says only that raw information should be provided as context 'once your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable... to ensure there is no information loss' — which is the monolithic brute-force pattern WebWeaver ablates AGAINST, and is consistent with Tavily's low readability score. No outline, no section-wise writing, no critique loop disclosed.
SYSTEM: Tavily
SOURCE GIVEN: https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en
STATED CONFIDENCE: medium

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim against the primary sources.

input.prompt: Extract the full content of this blog post. I need to know: (1) any DeepResearch Bench scores mentioned and what leaderboard/judge they refer to; (2) exactly what it says about Advanced Search / content chunks, global state persistence, source deduplication, information scope narrowing, token reduction percentage vs Open Deep Research, "guard-railed freedom"; (3) EVERYTHING it says about how the final report is composed/written — outline, section-wise writing, critique loop, or providing raw information as context to prevent information loss. Quote verbatim where possible.

input.query: Tavily DeepResearch Bench SOTA research agent leaderboard score

content.content:
# Blog Post Content Extraction

## (1) DeepResearch Bench Scores

The post mentions achieving "SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" but does not provide specific numerical scores. It references the "[DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard)" on Hugging Face as the evaluation source.

## (2) Advanced Search, Context Management & Token Reduction

**Advanced Search & Content Chunks:**
The post states that "Using Tavily's Advanced Search is the natural first step" and describes it as returning "only the most relevant content chunks from each source."

**Global State & Deduplication:**
The piece emphasizes that "global state persistence and source deduplication is paramount, and in our case, it helps threefold: (1) It ensures the agent is exposed only to fresh information. (2) It allows the engineer to recognize when the information scope is narrowing and to prompt the agent to explore untapped relevant domains. (3) It lends to effective source attribution later on."

**Token Reduction:**
The authors achieved a "66% reduction in token consumption" compared to Open Deep Research while maintaining state-of-the-art performance.

**Guard-railed Freedom:**
The post describes this as giving LLMs "guard-railed freedom to reason and iterate" to produce optimal results while managing non-determinism through safeguards.

## (3) Final Report Composition

The blog post does **not** explain the final report-writing process in detail. It only mentions that "only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss"—implying raw sources are provided at the end but offering no specifics about outlining, sectional writing, critique loops, or composition methodology.

content.text: The blog quotes check out. Now let me verify the leaderboard numbers and their currency.

input.prompt: List all entries on the leaderboard(s), including tavily-research. What are the RACE overall / comprehensiveness / insight / instruction-following / readability scores? Is there more than one leaderboard (e.g. a legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judged board and a newer GPT-5.5 judged board)? Which board does tavily-research appear on?

input.query: DeepResearch Bench leaderboard GPT-5.5 judge RACE replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro

content.content:
I cannot provide the information you requested based on the content supplied. The webpage excerpt shows only the header and navigation elements of a Hugging Face Space called "DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard," but it does not contain the actual leaderboard data, scores, or entries you're asking about.

The page appears to be loading ("Fetching metadata from the HF Docker repository... Refreshing"), so the leaderboard entries and their associated RACE scores (overall, comprehensiveness, insight, instruction-following, and readability) are not visible in this excerpt. To answer your questions about tavily-research's presence and scores, I would need access to the fully rendered leaderboard content.

Web search results for query: "DeepResearch Bench leaderboard GPT-5.5 judge RACE replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro"

Links: [{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"Gemini 2.5 Pro vs GPT-5.5 Pro: AI Benchmark Comparison 2026 | BenchLM.ai","url":"https://benchlm.ai/compare/gemini-2-5-pro-vs-gpt-5-5-pro"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"SWE-rebench Leaderboard","url":"https://swe-rebench.com/"},{"title":"FinDeepResearch: Evaluating Deep Research Agents in Rigorous Financial Analysis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.13936"},{"title":"LLM Benchmark Leaderboard 2026: Coding, Math, Reasoning and Agents | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/llm"},{"title":"Wiki Live Challenge: Challenging Deep Research Agents with Expert-Level Wikipedia Articles","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.01590"},{"title":"Planner-Centric Reinforcement Learning for Deep Research with Structure-Aware Reward","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30824"},{"title":"LLM Leaderboard 2026: Compare 300+ Top AI Models by Intelligence, Speed & Price","url":"https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/llm-leaderboard"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard and the transition from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 as the judge:

Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepResearch Bench benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = 68.78%).

All three candidates exceeded this baseline by 1.3–3 points; GPT-5.5 won on Overall, PAR, and FAS, and was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline).

From May 31, 2026 onwards, there was a dual-acceptance window for submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5), with results displayed on two separate leaderboards so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The judge-change context checks out. Now I need the actual scores. Let me hit the leaderboard data directly.

content.text: All four legacy numbers match exactly, and Tavily is indeed absent from the GPT-5.5 board. Let me pull the raw blog text myself to independently confirm there is no composition mechanism (rather than trusting a summarizer), and to check the SOTA wording.

input.command:
cd /tmp && curl -sL --max-time 60 -A "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 Chrome/122 Safari/537.36" "https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en" -o tav.html && echo "bytes: $(wc -c < tav.html)" && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re,html
t=open('/tmp/tav.html',encoding='utf-8',errors='replace').read()
t=re.sub(r'(?is)<(script|style)[^>]*>.*?</\1>',' ',t)
t=re.sub(r'(?i)</(p|div|h[1-6]|li|br)>','\n',t)
t=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>',' ',t)
t=html.unescape(t)
t=re.sub(r'[ \t]+',' ',t)
t=re.sub(r'\n\s*\n+','\n',t)
open('/tmp/tav.txt','w').write(t)
print("chars:",len(t))
kw=['SOTA','state-of-the-art','outline','section','critique','reflect','revis','draft','write','writing','compos','report','deliverable','information loss','66%','readab','judge','Gemini','GPT-5']
low=t.lower()
for k in kw:
    n=low.count(k.lower())
    print(f"{k!r:22} -> {n}")
EOF
input.description: Fetch raw blog and count composition-related keywords

content.text: `outline` appears once and `writing` twice — worth checking those in context before accepting "no outline disclosed."

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - <<'EOF'
t=open('/tmp/tav.txt',encoding='utf-8').read()
low=t.lower()
import re
for k in ['sota','state-of-the-art','outline','section','writing','reflect','deliverable','information loss','66%','judge']:
    for m in re.finditer(re.escape(k),low):
        s=max(0,m.start()-320); e=min(len(t),m.end()+320)
        print(f"\n----[{k.upper()}]----\n...{t[s:e].strip()}...")
EOF
input.description: Print keyword contexts from blog text

 product 4 min read 
 Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art
 Research agents are becoming a cornerstone of modern knowledge work. In this post, we share the technical and philosophical lessons behind building a state-of-the-art deep research agent—covering agent harness design, context engineering, and how we achieved dramatically lower token usage while reaching SOTA performance.
 By Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky
 November 24, 2025
 Research agents are rapidly becoming one of the most important applications of AI. Research is...

----[STATE-OF-THE-ART]----
...stantly, and scale effortlessly. Because of this, research agents are emerging as a top use case for AI today and will soon become a core subcomponent of broader agentic workflows across content generation, coding, sales, and more. In this post, we share the technical and philosophical lessons we’ve learned building a state-of-the-art research agent , and where we believe the field is headed.
 Building for the Future
 Agent Harness
 The task of building an agent harness is to create a software layer that enhances a model’s runtime execution through context management, tool invocations, loop control, orchestration, and error handling. Building appli...

----[OUTLINE]----
...Engineering — An Exercise in Curation
 Long-horizon research tasks expose a fundamental challenge in current agent design: the task of maintaining a clean, optimized context window over time. If curating context is not a task the engineer pays close attention to, the agent is almost destined for failure. The following outlines our thinking around this concept within the deep research domain.
 Context-Managed Web Retrieval
 Using Tavily’s Advanced Search is the natural first step to take in overcoming this challenge, in that it abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each sourc...

----[SECTION]----
...esses.
 Takeaways
 To build agents that improve over time, we followed a few guiding principles:
 Simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy.
 Pay close attention to what models and tools are being optimized for, and leverage their emerging capabilities.
 Focus on context engineering (more on this in the next section).
 Context Engineering — An Exercise in Curation
 Long-horizon research tasks expose a fundamental challenge in current agent design: the task of maintaining a clean, optimized context window over time. If curating context is not a task the engineer pays close attention to, the agent is almost destined for failure. Th...

----[SECTION]----
..., and when extrapolating this over a multi-agent system and with consumption at scale, the absolute value of tokens saved becomes even more significant.
 Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench – the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect.
 Building production-grade agents is a balancing act. We leaned into autonomy to maximize performance and quality, while still meeting strict requirements for latency, cost, and reliability.
 Engineering with Non-Determinism 
 LLMs are inherently non-deterministic, and we foun...

----[WRITING]----
...oken usage while reaching SOTA performance.
 By Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky
 November 24, 2025
 Research agents are rapidly becoming one of the most important applications of AI. Research is a foundational knowledge-work task: collecting, reading, and synthesizing information underpins everything from writing and decision-making to coding itself. Yet human-driven research is constrained by memory, reading speed, and time. AI research agents, by contrast, can process vast amounts of information, synthesize insights instantly, and scale effortlessly. Because of this, research agents are emerging as a top use case for AI toda...

----[WRITING]----
...lieve future models will be trained to solve the current pain points of agent developers. Every model is ultimately consumed by a harness, so models should evolve in service of that harness. We hope to see models improve in high-recall summarization (for context compression), tool-calling reliability, and concision in writing.
 Tools
 Similarly, tools should evolve to support LLMs and widely adopted agent harnesses. The best tools should perform some context engineering on the tool side, abstracted away from the agent. They should return only the most relevant data instead of dumping large volumes of tokens into the context window. As a to...

----[REFLECT]----
...de what to do next. Only once we’ve gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product.
 We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss.
 Doing More with Less
 This ap...

----[REFLECT]----
...thered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product.
 We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss.
 Doing More with Less
 This approach differs from traditional contex...

----[DELIVERABLE]----
...need. We next gather data from our sources, extracting the key insights and holding them in short-term memory, letting these distilled thoughts guide our subsequent actions.
 This cycle repeats: collect information, distill it, decide what to do next. Only once we’ve gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product.
 We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller....

----[DELIVERABLE]----
...d product.
 We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss.
 Doing More with Less
 This approach differs from traditional context structuring in a ReAct agent-based architecture. Typically, tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retr...

----[INFORMATION LOSS]----
...at tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss.
 Doing More with Less
 This approach differs from traditional context structuring in a ReAct agent-based architecture. Typically, tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retrieved/generated tokens being persisted in the context window on each subsequent iteration. This pat...

----[66%]----
...When comparing the two approaches, tokens are saved on a per-agent basis by a factor of (m+1)/2 , and when extrapolating this over a multi-agent system and with consumption at scale, the absolute value of tokens saved becomes even more significant.
 Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench – the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect.
 Building production-grade agents is a balancing act. We leaned into autonomy to maximize performance and quality, while still meeting strict requirements for latency, cost, and...

----[JUDGE]----
...e tempted to over-engineer by adding many tools that seemed useful in theory, but in practice this created new failure modes and made it harder for LLMs to consistently choose the right tool and iterate effectively.
 Evals 
 We used evals to steer our development process but also recognize their shortcomings. LLM-as-a-judge evals are difficult to trust: current models are non‑deterministic, uninterpretable in their reasoning, and can turn into bottlenecks, especially for long‑running agents where a single experiment can take days to complete.
 Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback. The core qu...

content.text: Everything checks out against primary sources, and the raw text reveals one thing the claim gets importantly wrong. Let me pin down the blog's date and Tavily's current rank.

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - <<'EOF'
import csv
for f,label in [('legacy.csv','LEGACY (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge)'),('gpt55.csv','GPT-5.5 judge (current)')]:
    rows=list(csv.DictReader(open(f)))
    print(f"\n=== {label}: {len(rows)} entries")
    hit=[(i+1,r) for i,r in enumerate(rows) if 'tavily' in r['model'].lower()]
    if not hit:
        print("  tavily-research: ABSENT")
        print(f"  leader: {rows[0]['model']} = {rows[0]['overall_score']}")
        continue
    i,r=hit[0]
    print(f"  tavily-research RANK {i}/{len(rows)}  overall={r['overall_score']}")
    print(f"    comp={r['comprehensiveness']} insight={r['insight']} IF={r['instruction_following']} read={r['readability']}")
    print(f"    weakest dim = readability? {min([('comp',float(r['comprehensiveness'])),('insight',float(r['insight'])),('IF',float(r['instruction_following'])),('read',float(r['readability']))],key=lambda x:x[1])}")
    print(f"  current leader: {rows[0]['model']} = {rows[0]['overall_score']}  (gap to tavily: +{float(rows[0]['overall_score'])-float(r['overall_score']):.2f})")
    print(f"  entries ABOVE tavily: {i-1}")
EOF
input.description: Compute tavily rank and dim ordering on both boards

content.content:
=== LEGACY (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge): 45 entries
  tavily-research RANK 23/45  overall=52.44
    comp=52.84 insight=53.59 IF=51.92 read=49.21
    weakest dim = readability? ('read', 49.21)
  current leader: qianfan_deepresearch_0430 = 58.03  (gap to tavily: +5.59)
  entries ABOVE tavily: 22

=== GPT-5.5 judge (current): 10 entries

  leader: cellcog-max = 55.78

content.text:
Verification complete. Every element checks out against primary sources — except the lead assertion.

**What I confirmed (primary sources):**

1. **All five scores match the live leaderboard exactly.** I pulled the raw CSVs the HF Space renders from (`huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard`, files `data/leaderboard.csv` and `data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv`). `tavily-research` = 52.44 / comp 52.84 / insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / read 49.21. Readability is indeed its weakest dimension. It is **absent from the GPT-5.5 board** (10 entries, led by cellcog-max at 55.78). The two-board structure (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judge vs. new GPT-5.5 judge) is real and is exactly what the dual `data/` + `data_gpt55/` directories encode.

2. **Every blog quote is verbatim.** I scraped and stripped the raw HTML rather than trusting a summarizer. "only the most relevant content chunks from each source"; "global state persistence and source deduplication is paramount"; "recognize when the information scope is narrowing"; "reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench"; "guard-railed freedom"; and the composition line — "it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss."

3. **The "no composition mechanism" finding is stronger than claimed.** Keyword counts over the full stripped text: `critique` 0, `compos*` 0, `draft` 0, `revis*` 0, **`report` 0**. The single `outline` hit is "The following **outlines our thinking**" — prose, not a report outline. The two `section` hits are "the next section" (the blog's own sections) and a nav artifact. The blog never once uses the word "report." The claim's negative assertion holds.

**The defect — the lead assertion is stale.** The blog is dated **November 24, 2025**, and at that time 52.44 plausibly did top the board. But *today* `tavily-research` is **rank 23 of 45** on the legacy board, 5.59 points behind the leader (qianfan at 58.03), sitting below MindDR-V1.5 and above thinkdepthai. It is not SOTA and has not been re-submitted under the current GPT-5.5 evaluator. Any downstream sentence of the form "Tavily is SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" would be false as written — which is precisely the staleness trap flagged in the task. The 22 systems above it are all later submissions.

Net: the claim's *actionable* conclusion is correct and well-founded — the write-up is a retrieval/context-economy story with no disclosed composition mechanism, and the monolithic "dump raw sources at the end" pattern is consistent with its 49.21 readability. The recommendation to stop spending time on it stands. But the framing "SOTA claim is real" must be time-boxed to Nov 2025, not asserted in the present tense.

input.reasoning:
Verified against primary sources on both axes.

SCORES — CONFIRMED EXACTLY. I bypassed the JS-rendered HF Space and pulled the raw CSVs it renders from (huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard, files data/leaderboard.csv [legacy, Gemini-2.5-Pro judge] and data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv [new GPT-5.5 judge]). tavily-research = 52.44 overall / comp 52.84 / insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / read 49.21 — all four sub-scores match the DETAIL to the decimal. Readability IS its weakest dimension (49.21, lowest of the four). It IS absent from the GPT-5.5 board (10 entries, led by cellcog-max 55.78). The dual-board structure described in the task prompt is real and is literally encoded in the two data directories.

BLOG QUOTES — CONFIRMED VERBATIM. I scraped the raw HTML and stripped tags myself rather than trusting a summarizer. All quoted strings appear verbatim: "only the most relevant content chunks from each source"; "global state persistence and source deduplication is paramount" (with the threefold benefit, incl. "recognize when the information scope is narrowing"); "reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench"; "guard-railed freedom to reason and iterate"; and the composition line "it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss."

NO-COMPOSITION-MECHANISM — CONFIRMED, AND STRONGER THAN CLAIMED. Keyword counts over the full stripped text (10,845 chars): critique 0, compos* 0, draft 0, revis* 0, and report 0 — the blog never uses the word "report" at all. The single "outline" hit is "The following outlines our thinking" (prose verb, not a report outline). The two "section" hits are the blog's own section references. There is no outline stage, no section-wise writing, no critique/reflection-on-draft loop. The claim's negative assertion is fully supported. The disclosed pattern is exactly the monolithic "dump all raw sources into context at write time" approach, consistent with the low 49.21 readability.

WHY STALE, NOT CONFIRMED: the claim's lead assertion — "Tavily's SOTA claim is real" — is stated in the present tense and is no longer true. This is precisely the staleness trap the task warned about. Everything else in the claim survives adversarial checking, and its actionable conclusion (engineering/token-efficiency story, not a report-writing story; no usable composition mechanism; stop spending time) is correct and well-founded.
input.corrected:
Tavily's SOTA claim was true only as of the blog's publication date, November 24, 2025 (byline: Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky). It is NOT true today. On the live legacy leaderboard, tavily-research at 52.44 now ranks 23rd of 45 entries, 5.59 points behind the leader qianfan_deepresearch_0430 (58.03); the 22 systems above it are all later submissions (e.g. ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 57.27, zhipu_deep_research 57.06, cellcog-max 56.67). It sits just below MindDR-V1.5 (52.54). It has never been re-submitted under the current GPT-5.5 RACE evaluator, so it is absent from the current board (led by cellcog-max at 55.78).

Any downstream statement of the form "Tavily is SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" is false as written and must be time-boxed to Nov 2025. Note the blog itself hedges on benchmarks anyway: "Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback," and "LLM-as-a-judge evals are difficult to trust."

Sources (primary): https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en (raw HTML, dated November 24, 2025); https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv and .../data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (retrieved 2026-07-13).

The rest of the claim stands: no outline, no section-wise writing, no critique loop is disclosed anywhere in the write-up, and the recommendation not to spend further time on it is sound.
